<|det|>header [425, 24, 671, 37]<|/det|>CIVIL HISTORY, 1603-1640.
<|det|>header [896, 24, 946, 37]<|/det|>1610.
<|det|>text [158, 46, 954, 206]<|/det|>since 1514, \( ^{1} \) and which had been applied to English merchant ships since 1553, was still untried in the Navy, possibly because it had been found to set up galvanic action with the iron of the rudder pintles, etc. Nor was Hawkmys's sheathing of double planks, with tar and hair between them, as generally adopted as it should have been, though it remained in some favour until late in the seventeenth century. Much more care, in fact, was devoted to making ships look well than to making them really serviceable; and the result often was that after her completion a vessel was found to be so crank that it was deemed necessary to "fur" or "girdle" her with a partial, or even an entire external planking beyond her original skin. \( ^{2} \)
<|det|>text [158, 206, 954, 472]<|/det|>As the Royal Prince \( ^{3} \) was the greatest constructive effort of the reign of James I., some account must be here given of her. Her crew was laid at Woolwich on October 20th 1608, the chief constructor being Phineas Pett. Her nominal tonnage was 1200, but, measured according to the rules in force in 1632, it was 1035 nett, and 1330 gross. After the work had been some time in progress, and had met with much adverse criticism from rival shipwrights, a commission, consisting of Captain George Waymouth, Matthew Baker (who had been a principal consenter for more than half a century), William Bright, Edward Stevens, and others, was ordered to report on what was being done. Pett hated Waymouth, and Baker despised the Pett family, while Bright was particularly jealous of Phineas. But Pett had powerful protectors in the Lord High Admiral and Sir Robert Mansell; and after there had been not only inquiry and further inquiry, but also a special scrutiny by the King and Prince Henry, Phineas Pett emerged triumphant. An attempt to launch the ship was made on September 24th, 1610, but it failed, owing to the dockhead being too narrow to allow her to pass. She was, however, successfully launched a little later.*
<|det|>text [160, 474, 954, 519]<|/det|>Pett, on this occasion, owed more to his protectors than to the merits of his work, for the Royal Prince, though a striking object in the water, was both ill-designed and ill-built. As early as 1621, it was reported to Buckingham \( ^{5} \) that although she had cost
<|det|>list [230, 521, 942, 655]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_footnote [230, 521, 911, 534]<|/det|>\( ^{1} \)  Duro's Armada Español, 121.
<|det|>page_footnote [230, 534, 903, 546]<|/det|>\( ^{2} \)  Harl. MSS. 2361: ‘Nomenclature Variables’ (Manwaring’s ‘Dictionary’).
<|det|>page_footnote [230, 546, 671, 557]<|/det|>\( ^{3} \)  Often called Prince Royal, or simply Prince.
<|det|>page_footnote [230, 557, 645, 568]<|/det|>\( ^{4} \)  Oppenheim, ‘Admin.’ of Royal Navy’, 204.
<|det|>page_footnote [230, 568, 421, 579]<|/det|>\( ^{5} \)  Coke MSS. i. 114.
<PAGE><|det|>page_number [40, 24, 77, 37]<|/det|>1625.]
<|det|>header [223, 24, 671, 37]<|/det|>THE WITHDRAWAL FROM CADIZ.
<|det|>page_number [788, 24, 814, 37]<|/det|>63
<|det|>text [37, 46, 819, 122]<|/det|>he found that the merchantmen had kept out of danger, and that the Dutch had lost two ships. When such conduct passed unpunished, it was easy to foresee what support could be counted on. Wimbledon gave further orders; the troops landed; Sir John Bright summoned gave forth, and, fortunately, it surrendered:—fortunately, because, as afterwards appeared, the troops had not even brought scaling-ladders ashore.
<|det|>text [37, 122, 819, 332]<|/det|>No attempt had been made to pursue the ships up the harbour. Wimbledon had been told that there was not enough water, and had asked no further. He did not consider that Drake had sailed into the harbour before him, and that he might have passed up, as Drake had passed, with the lead going on both sides. Throughout he ignored his commission. Instead of burning the Spanish ships, fifty-nine sail in all, as he had been ordered, he landed troops and marched towards Guanzillo's. Arrived there he found that his troops had no food—no meat had been landed—and he stupidly marched them into the village where was stored the wine for the West India fleets. The whole army at once drank itself into a state of madness, and all that Wimbledon could do was to decide that the bridge was not worth holding, or even destroying, and to march his troops back again. Yet this bridge has principally occurred in the past he held by any one who would hold the town, for it was the only means of access from the mainland to Leon Island, on which Cádiz lies. All that Wimbledon did was, to take Fort Puntal and evacuate it, to take the bridge and abandon it, and to capture a few boats from St. Mary. Yet the few men who protected theirs in the Lord High School passing on foot are not the town; for it was the only means of access from the mainland to Leon Island, on which Cádiz lies. All that Wimbledon did was, to take Fort Puntal, and evacuate it, to take the bridge and abandon it, and to capture a few boats from St. Mary. Fort Puntal to replace the long boats which had been lost on the rough passage out.
<|det|>text [37, 332, 819, 422]<|/det|>It was now decided to look for the Plate fleet, and, on November 4th, Wimbledon's squadrons took up their positions in a long line stretching south from Cape St. Vincent. They might have saved themselves the trouble and they known that the fleet had taken the southern route and, escaping to an isle, had reached Cadiz two days after the English had left it.
<|det|>text [37, 422, 819, 485]<|/det|>Wimbledon had intended to cruise till the 20th, but on the 16th he was persuaded to bear up for England. His ships were fast and leaking, the crews were dying fast. Burchett's tells a story that,
<|det|>list [202, 491, 625, 551]<|/det|>
<|det|>page_footnote [202, 491, 365, 504]<|/det|>* 'Travers' p. 275;
<|det|>page_footnote [205, 504, 625, 516]<|/det|>* Called the bridge of Savez, in vol. I, 341, 545.
<|det|>page_footnote [205, 516, 353, 528]<|/det|>* 'Travers' p. 275.

<|det|>header [366, 28, 728, 42]<|/det|>MILITARY HISTORY, 1603-1649.
<|det|>header [879, 28, 932, 42]<|/det|>[1627.
<|det|>text [161, 49, 934, 96]<|/det|>sent home to hasten the Irish regiments and the supplies which had been promised. There was also a report abroad \( ^{1} \) that a fleet of forty Spanish warships and twenty Dungenquerries would soon arrive to help the French in holding the island. \( ^{2} \)
<|det|>text [160, 96, 934, 262]<|/det|>For two days Buckingham busied himself in landing guns, but had his determination. He did not consider the Dutch had sailed, and took the bowbord before him, and that he might have passed up, as Drake had passed, with the lead going on both sides. Throughout he ignored his commission. Instead of burning the Spaniish ships, fifty-nine sail in all, as he had been ordered, he landed troops and marched towards Guzmán bridge. Arrived there he found that his troops had no food—no food had been landed—and he stupidly marched them into the village where was stored the wine for the West India fleets. The whole army at once drank itself into a steadyed madness, and all that Wimbledon could do was to decide that the bridge was not worth holding, or even destroying, and to march his troops back again. Yet this bridge was a principle for deceit that must be held by any one who would hold the town, for it was the only means of access from the mainland to Leon Island, on which Cádiz lies. All that Winsboldon did was, to take Fort Pinatal and evacuate it, to take the bridge and abandon it, and to capture a few boats from St. Marys, to even replace the long boats which had been lost on the rough passage out.
<|det|>text [40, 262, 752, 360]<|/det|>It was now decided to look for the Plate fleet, and, on November 4th, Winsbolden's squadrons took up their positions in a long line stretching south from Cape St. Vincent. They might have saved themselves the trouble had they known that the fleet had taken the southern route and, escaping to the shore, had reached Cadiz two days after the English had left it.
<|det|>text [40, 360, 752, 404]<|/det|>Wimbledon had intended to cruise till the 20th, but on the 16th he was persuaded to bear up for England. His ships were fast and leading, the crews were dying fast. Burchett's tells a story that,

<|det|>page_number [795, 28, 834, 41]<|/det|>227
<|det|>text [55, 49, 855, 97]<|/det|>NOTE.—The list is merely a tentative one. These do not appear to be new in evidence, material or compelling since which could be pronounced to be absolutely complete. Yet it is believed that the long catalogue below, which has been prepared almost entirely from contemporary M. S. authorities, will be found much more nearly perfect than anything of the kind that has been previously attempted.

<|det|>text [80, 842, 862, 864]<|/det|>davies, wind, and then false reports of the presence of a large French squadron near the Strait, delayed the fleet, which, by July 17th, had not advanced eastward of Tetuan. In consequence of suggestions which had been sent to him through the English minister at Lisbon for King George Charles of Spain and the King of Portugal, Rooke on that day held another council of war, which,
<|det|>text [80, 865, 862, 903]<|/det|>after discarding various projects, determined to make a sudden attempt on Gibraltar, which at the time was held in the interests of the French candidate for the Spanish crown. The plan of action involved the landing to the northward of the town of the English and Dutch Marines, under the Prince of Hessen, who would sever

<|det|>header [366, 28, 744, 42]<|/det|>MINOR OPERATIONS, 1660–1714.
<|det|>header [875, 28, 926, 42]<|/det|>[1764.
<|det|>text [145, 50, 929, 98]<|/det|>detached force rejoined Vice-Admiral Neville, who had proceeded to sea in search of it. The squadron then headed for Jamaica, and thence steered for Havana, before which city it arrived on July 22nd. On the 20th, Captain Mees had died of fever on board his ship, the 4th.
<|det|>text [145, 99, 931, 253]<|/det|>At Havana, Neville found the galleons. The men of the squadron were  \( \frac{1}{4} \)  early, and was not employed to reach them. He found the French in the Mediterranean. Rear-Admiral Byng was first, however, detached to Cadiz to effect an exchange of prisoners.
